what_changed: Five criteria produced widely different scores; information form scored highest and predictive form lowest, indicating criteria behave as task-specific tools rather than interchangeable definitions.
Higher removal fractions increased overshoot and delta_C monotonically, with system collapse emerging only at 50% removal.
Shifts between object-level and mapping-level explanation followed a repeating cycle rather than a one-directional hierarchy.
All eight measurement variants produced significant results with high correlation, but the absolute level of the estimate varied while the ranking stayed stable.
The same underlying system produced different detected features depending on how the data was encoded, with data format exerting more influence than system identity.
Every single audit showed a shift in interpretation when methodology changed, with zero shifts under a fixed methodology.
Every observed shift was accompanied by element links, and no shift occurred in their absence.
Audits split sharply into a small number of failures and a large group where lower-order properties were fully retained within higher-order forms.
The measured quantity was binary—always one of two values—and only changed when a specific estimator parameter (bin count) was altered.
Four transition points could not exist independently; removing an earlier one prevented or weakened later ones, forming a sequential dependency chain.
Three models of what objects are scored differently, with the element-link model scoring highest and the experience model explaining the most phenomena.
Four criteria succeeded on every test system while the causal criterion failed 60% of the time, breaking under random dependencies.
Difference detection emerged in about half of generated systems, and ablation showed it depended critically on nonlinearity, feedback, and memory.
Eleven different construction paths all produced the same four transition points, confirming the points are reachable from any starting vocabulary.
All seven component combinations showed steady behavior across recursion depths, with layering preserved in every condition.
Shift occurred in 100% of audits where actual element links and consistency were present, but adding newness or retention to the combination did not change the outcome.
The same basic forces operated across all transitions, but the element graph grew in nodes and edges with each step, enabling new capabilities.
At every transition, a vast pool of differences was available but only a tiny subset was retained, with the retention mechanism being what determined the capability that emerged.
Among 1200 rule systems, only 18 met all criteria, with the best composite score reaching 0.73 and specific properties appearing at different recursion depths.
Changing density effectively altered structural measurements but had no significant effect on the primary variable of interest.
